sections.title: Overview
sections.content: This guideline provides a practical matrix for when FIFOEMS physios should manage, seek doctor input, involve paramedics, refer to hospital, or recommend medevac.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- New patient assessment with uncertain stability
- Worsening symptoms during follow-up
- Need to choose between local conservative care and higher-acuity pathway

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The purpose is to shorten delay, not add bureaucracy
- The same patient may need more than one escalation pathway, for example doctor plus paramedic plus hospital transfer
- Environment, distance, weather, and staffing can change the threshold for medevac

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- GP / doctor: use for non-emergent diagnostic clarification, medication needs, work capacity disputes, persistent symptoms, or red-flag but stable presentations
- Paramedic: use for acute deterioration, urgent assessment support, analgesia needs, transport decisions, or medical instability
- ED / hospital: use for suspected fracture, major neuro deficit, chest pain, serious infection, acute swelling/vascular threat, cauda equina, concussion red flags, or inability to cope safely onsite
- Medevac: use when time-critical care or imaging is needed and site capability or surface transport is inadequate

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Build local site pathways so clinicians know who to call first in each scenario
- Reassess after escalation; some apparently stable presentations can deteriorate while waiting
- Document the reason for escalation, who was contacted, advice received, and any delay or transfer constraints

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Good escalation is specific: who, why, how urgent, and what risks exist if the patient remains onsite
